title: Linear search vs binary search
description: Implement both linear search and binary search and compare their results

Step 2: i=1, arr[1]=7 ≠ 9 → continue
Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=2 ≠ 9 → continue
Step 4: i=3, arr[3]=9 == 9 → found at index 3, return 3

Step 3: i=2, arr[2]=6 ≠ 3 → continue

approaches.name: Approach 1: Linear Search (Brute Force)
approaches.summary: Iterate through each element in the array sequentially to find the target value. Use this when the array is unsorted or small, as it works for any order but is inefficient for large datasets.

ladders.desc: Complete implementation of linear search with comparison to binary search

ladders.title: Building Block — Array Indexing and Value Comparison
ladders.desc: Access array elements and compare values
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses on two fundamental operations: accessing elements in an array using indices, and comparing values for equality. The intuition is that arrays store elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via a numerical index starting from 0. To search, we need to retrieve each element and check if it matches our target. The algorithm: 1) Understand that arr[i] gives the value at position i. 2) Compare this value with target using equality operator (==). 3) Based on comparison result, take appropriate action. Time complexity for a single access and comparison is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This connects to the sequential checking logic by providing the basic operations performed in each iteration of the search loop. Without proper understanding of array indexing (0-based vs 1-based) and value comparison, the search algorithm cannot function correctly. Common mistakes include: confusing array indices (thinking arr[1] is first element), using assignment (=) instead of comparison (==), and not understanding that comparison returns a boolean value. These operations form the foundation of not just search algorithms but most array processing tasks.

Pseudocode:
# Access element at index i
value = arr[i]

# Compare with target
if value == target:
    # Do something when equal
else:
    # Do something when not equal

examples.trace:
Step 1: Access arr[1] → value = 20
Step 2: Compare value (20) with target (20) → 20 == 20 is True
Step 3: Since comparison is True, we have a match

Step 1: Access arr[0] → value = 10
Step 2: Compare value (10) with target (25) → 10 == 25 is False
Step 3: Since comparison is False, no match

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Iterating Through Array Indices
ladders.desc: Loop through all valid positions in an array
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation involves creating a loop that visits each position in an array exactly once, in sequential order. The intuition is that arrays have a fixed number of elements, and we can use a counter variable to track our current position, incrementing it until we've visited all positions. The algorithm: 1) Determine array length n. 2) Initialize counter i to 0 (first index). 3) While i is less than n, we have valid positions to visit. 4) Perform operation at position i. 5) Increment i to move to next position. 6) Repeat until i equals n (all positions visited). Time complexity is O(n) to visit n positions. Space complexity is O(1) for the counter variable. This connects to array indexing by providing the mechanism to systematically access every element—without this iteration, we could only access individual positions. Common mistakes include: starting at 1 instead of 0, using i <= n instead of i < n (causes out-of-bounds error), and not understanding that array indices are integers. This iteration pattern is one of the most fundamental in programming, appearing in virtually all algorithms that process collections of data.

Pseudocode:
function iterate_array(arr):
    n = length(arr)
    for i from 0 to n-1:
        # Do something with arr[i]
    # All positions visited

examples.trace:
Step 1: n = 3, i = 0 (valid: 0 < 3) → visit index 0
Step 2: i = 1 (valid: 1 < 3) → visit index 1
Step 3: i = 2 (valid: 2 < 3) → visit index 2
Step 4: i = 3 (invalid: 3 < 3 is false) → stop
Step 1: n = 0, i = 0 (invalid: 0 < 0 is false) → stop immediately

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Arrays and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic array structure and position numbering
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept introduces arrays as ordered collections of elements where each element has a numerical position called an index. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store multiple values under one name, with each value accessible by its position number. Arrays have: 1) A fixed size (number of elements). 2) Elements of the same type (typically). 3) Indexing starting at 0 (first element at position 0, second at 1, etc.). 4) Contiguous storage in memory. To work with arrays, you need to understand: how to declare/create an array, how to access elements using bracket notation (arr[index]), and that valid indices range from 0 to length-1. Time and space complexity aren't applicable at this basic level—this is about understanding the data structure itself. This connects to array iteration by establishing what we're iterating over—without understanding that arrays have indices and elements, iteration makes no sense. Common beginner mistakes include: thinking indices start at 1, trying to access indices outside the valid range, and confusing arrays with other data structures. This foundation is essential for all array-based algorithms, not just search.

Pseudocode:
# Creating an array
arr = [value1, value2, value3, ...]

# Accessing elements
first_element = arr[0]   # Index 0 = first position
second_element = arr[1]  # Index 1 = second position

# Array length
n = length(arr)  # Number of elements

examples.trace: Array has 3 elements: position 0 contains 10, position 1 contains 20, position 2 contains 30. Valid indices are 0, 1, 2. arr[3] would be invalid (out of bounds).
Array has 1 element: position 0 contains 5. Valid indices are only 0. arr[1] would be invalid.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Binary Search (Divide and Conquer)
approaches.summary: Repeatedly divide the sorted array in half by comparing the target to the middle element, eliminating half of the remaining elements each step. Use this for sorted arrays to achieve much faster search times than linear methods.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Implement and Compare Linear vs Binary Search
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of both search algorithms with comparison analysis
ladders.explanation: This full problem requires implementing both linear search and binary search algorithms, then comparing their performance characteristics. Linear search works by sequentially checking each element in an array until the target is found or all elements are examined. Binary search requires a sorted array and works by repeatedly dividing the search interval in half. The intuition behind binary search is that by comparing the target to the middle element, we can eliminate half of the remaining elements each iteration, making it exponentially faster than linear search for large datasets. The step-by-step algorithm: 1) For linear search: iterate through array, compare each element to target, return index if found, else -1. 2) For binary search: initialize left=0, right=n-1; while left ≤ right: calculate mid = (left+right)//2; if arr[mid]==target: return mid; elif arr[mid]<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return -1. Time analysis: linear search O(n), binary search O(log n). Space analysis: both O(1). Connection to approach: This implements the complete binary search approach described. Common mistakes: forgetting to sort array before binary search, integer overflow in mid calculation, off-by-one errors in bounds. Real code pattern: def linear_search(arr, target): for i in range(len(arr)): if arr[i]==target: return i; return -1; def binary_search(arr, target): left, right=0, len(arr)-1; while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; if arr[mid]==target: return mid; elif arr[mid]<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: Linear search: Check index 0: 1≠7; index 1: 3≠7; index 2: 5≠7; index 3: 7==7 → return 3. Binary search: Array sorted. left=0, right=5, mid=2: arr[2]=5<7 → left=3; left=3, right=5, mid=4: arr[4]=9>7 → right=3; left=3, right=3, mid=3: arr[3]=7==7 → return 3.

Linear search: Check index 0: 2≠5; index 1: 4≠5; index 2: 6≠5; index 3: 8≠5; index 4: 10≠5 → return -1. Binary search: Array sorted. left=0, right=4, mid=2: arr[2]=6>5 → right=1; left=0, right=1, mid=0: arr[0]=2<5 → left=1; left=1, right=1, mid=1: arr[1]=4<5 → left=2; left=2>right=1 → return -1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Binary Search Implementation
ladders.desc: Implement binary search algorithm on a sorted array
ladders.explanation: This key sub-routine implements the binary search algorithm, which is the core of Approach 2. The intuition is that by maintaining a search interval and repeatedly comparing the target to the middle element, we can eliminate half of the remaining elements each iteration. This works because the array is sorted, so if the target is less than the middle element, it must be in the left half (if present), and if greater, in the right half. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize left=0, right=n-1. 2) While left ≤ right: calculate mid = floor((left+right)/2). 3) If arr[mid]==target: return mid. 4) If arr[mid]<target: set left=mid+1. 5) Else: set right=mid-1. 6) If loop ends, return -1. Time: O(log n) as we halve search space each iteration. Space: O(1) using only pointers. Connection to L0: This is the binary search component of the full comparison problem. Common mistakes: Using < instead of ≤ in loop condition (misses single-element case), integer overflow in mid calculation (use left+(right-left)//2), forgetting array must be sorted. Real code pattern: def binary_search(arr, target): left, right=0, len(arr)-1; while left<=right: mid=left+(right-left)//2; if arr[mid]==target: return mid; elif arr[mid]<target: left=mid+1; else: right=mid-1; return -1.

examples.trace: left=0, right=9, mid=4: arr[4]=5<7 → left=5; left=5, right=9, mid=7: arr[7]=8>7 → right=6; left=5, right=6, mid=5: arr[5]=6<7 → left=6; left=6, right=6, mid=6: arr[6]=7==7 → return 6.
left=0, right=4, mid=2: arr[2]=30>25 → right=1; left=0, right=1, mid=0: arr[0]=10<25 → left=1; left=1, right=1, mid=1: arr[1]=20<25 → left=2; left=2>right=1 → return -1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Divide and Conquer Search Pattern
ladders.desc: Implement the divide-and-conquer pattern of comparing target to middle element
ladders.explanation: This core logic implements the fundamental divide-and-conquer pattern of binary search. The intuition is that by comparing the target value to the middle element of a sorted range, we can determine which half of the range to search next, effectively dividing the problem size in half each iteration. This works because in a sorted array, all elements to the left of the middle are ≤ the middle element, and all to the right are ≥ it. Step-by-step: 1) Given sorted array arr and indices left, right defining current search range. 2) Calculate mid index = (left+right)//2. 3) Compare target to arr[mid]. 4) If equal: found target. 5) If target < arr[mid]: search left half (left to mid-1). 6) If target > arr[mid]: search right half (mid+1 to right). Time: Each comparison reduces search space by half, leading to O(log n) complexity. Space: O(1) for indices. Connection to L1: This is the algorithmic pattern used in binary search implementation. Common mistakes: Incorrect mid calculation causing infinite loops, not updating bounds correctly after comparison. Real code pattern: while left<=right: mid=(left+right)//2; if target==arr[mid]: return mid; elif target<arr[mid]: right=mid-1; else: left=mid+1.

examples.trace: Initial: left=0, right=4. Iteration 1: mid=2, arr[2]=5==target → found at index 2.
Initial: left=0, right=4. Iteration 1: mid=2, arr[2]=6<7 → left=3; left=3, right=4, mid=3: arr[3]=8>7 → right=2; left=3>right=2 → not found.

ladders.title: Building Block — Finding Middle Index in a Range
ladders.desc: Calculate the middle index between two bounds without overflow
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on correctly calculating the middle index between two bounds, a fundamental operation in binary search. The intuition is that we need to find the midpoint between left and right indices to divide the search range. The naive approach (left+right)//2 can cause integer overflow for very large arrays. The safe method uses left + (right-left)//2, which gives the same result without overflow risk. Step-by-step: 1) Given left and right indices. 2) Calculate mid = left + (right-left)//2. 3) This gives the floor of the average. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This middle index calculation is used in each iteration of the divide-and-conquer pattern. Common mistakes: Using (left+right)/2 without integer division, not considering overflow for large indices. Real code pattern: mid = left + (right-left)//2.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Elements in Sorted Array
ladders.desc: Compare a target value to an array element and determine search direction
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing a target value to an element in a sorted array and determining whether to search left or right. The intuition is that in a sorted array, the comparison result tells us exactly which half of the array could contain the target. If target < element, target must be in left half (if present). If target > element, target must be in right half. If equal, target found. Step-by-step: 1) Given sorted array arr, index i, target value. 2) Compare target to arr[i]. 3) If target == arr[i]: return 'found'. 4) If target < arr[i]: return 'left'. 5) If target > arr[i]: return 'right'. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison determines how to update bounds after calculating middle index. Common mistakes: Not handling equality case separately, incorrect inequality directions. Real code pattern: if target==arr[i]: return 'found'; elif target<arr[i]: return 'left'; else: return 'right'.

examples.trace: arr[2]=5, target=5 → equal → 'found'.
arr[2]=6, target=7 → target > element → 'right'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Sorted Arrays
ladders.desc: Identify and work with sorted arrays
ladders.explanation: This foundation concept introduces sorted arrays, which are essential for binary search. A sorted array is one where elements are arranged in non-decreasing order (each element is ≥ the previous one). The intuition is that this ordering property allows efficient search algorithms like binary search to work. In a sorted array, we can make assumptions about element positions: if we know an element's value, we know all elements to its left are ≤ it and all to its right are ≥ it. Step-by-step to check if array is sorted: 1) Start at index 1. 2) For each index i, check if arr[i] ≥ arr[i-1]. 3) If all checks pass, array is sorted. Time: O(n) to verify. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: Binary search comparisons rely on the array being sorted. Common mistakes: Confusing sorted with strictly increasing (can have duplicates), not checking entire array. Real code pattern: def is_sorted(arr): for i in range(1,len(arr)): if arr[i]<arr[i-1]: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Check index 1: 3≥1 ✓; index 2: 5≥3 ✓; index 3: 7≥5 ✓; index 4: 9≥7 ✓ → all pass → sorted.
Check index 1: 2≥5 ✗ → not sorted.
